title: Unsupported `cook` actions clear the pending solvency check, enabling uncollateralized borrows and collateral withdrawals
claim: `cook()` sets `status.needsSolvencyCheck = true` after `_borrow()` and `_removeCollateral()`, but `ACTION_ACCRUE` is declared without any matching branch. Any unrecognized action falls through to `_additionalCookAction()`, whose base implementation is empty and therefore returns a zeroed `CookStatus` that overwrites the live `status` struct and clears the pending solvency check before the final `_isSolvent()` gate runs.
impact: An attacker can batch a borrow or collateral withdrawal with `ACTION_ACCRUE` (or any other unsupported action) to skip the end-of-call solvency check. This allows draining available MIM with insufficient collateral or removing collateral from an already borrowed position, leaving the cauldron with directly realizable bad debt.
paths: `cook([ACTION_BORROW, ACTION_ACCRUE], ...)` -> `_borrow()` transfers MIM and marks `needsSolvencyCheck` -> `ACTION_ACCRUE` falls into `_additionalCookAction()` -> returned zeroed status clears the flag -> final solvency check is skipped

`cook([ACTION_REMOVE_COLLATERAL, ACTION_ACCRUE], ...)` -> `_removeCollateral()` transfers collateral and marks `needsSolvencyCheck` -> trailing unsupported action clears the flag -> borrower exits with debt still outstanding

title: Liquidation reverts when required seizure exceeds remaining collateral, leaving zero-collateral bad debt unresolved
claim: `liquidate()` chooses `borrowPart` only from `maxBorrowParts[i]` and the user's debt, computes the corresponding `collateralShare`, and then blindly subtracts that share from `userCollateralShare[user]`. It never clamps either `borrowPart` or `collateralShare` to the collateral that actually remains, so liquidation reverts whenever the computed seizure exceeds the user's balance.
impact: Bad debt can become impossible to clear through the normal liquidation path once a position has too little collateral left for the requested liquidation size. In the worst case, positions with debt but zero remaining collateral cannot be liquidated at all, so insolvency stays stuck in `totalBorrow` and cannot be socialized or cleaned up by liquidators.
paths: Severe undercollateralization or an F-001-assisted full collateral withdrawal leaves a user with debt and no collateral -> liquidator calls `liquidate()` with any positive `borrowPart` -> computed `collateralShare` is positive -> `userCollateralShare[user].sub(collateralShare)` underflows and reverts

A liquidator submits a larger `maxBorrowParts[i]` for a deeply underwater user -> the corresponding seizure exceeds remaining collateral -> the whole liquidation attempt for that user reverts instead of partially liquidating up to available collateral

title: Borrows and collateral withdrawals continue against stale cached prices whenever oracle updates fail
claim: `updateExchangeRate()` silently falls back to the cached `exchangeRate` whenever `oracle.get()` reports failure, and every solvency-gated borrow/withdraw path (`borrow`, `removeCollateral`, and the final `cook()` check) accepts that stale value. `init()` also seeds `exchangeRate` from `oracle.get()` without checking its success flag, so a fresh clone can start with a zero or otherwise invalid cached rate that remains in force until a later successful update.
impact: If the oracle becomes unavailable after the collateral price has moved against the protocol, users can keep borrowing or withdrawing collateral against an outdated, overly favorable price. In the worst case, a clone initialized during an oracle failure can begin with `exchangeRate == 0`, making any account with nonzero collateral appear solvent for essentially arbitrary borrowing until a valid oracle update finally lands.
paths: Collateral market moves down but `oracle.get()` starts returning `success = false` -> `updateExchangeRate()` reuses the last cached rate -> `borrow()` / `removeCollateral()` / `cook()` solvency checks still pass at the stale price -> attacker extracts excess MIM or collateral

A clone is initialized while `oracle.get()` fails or returns an invalid zero rate -> `init()` stores that unchecked rate -> subsequent failed `updateExchangeRate()` calls keep returning the poisoned cache -> a user adds dust collateral and borrows up to available liquidity

title: Privileged owner can assign debt to arbitrary users without transferring them any MIM
claim: `addBorrowPosition()` directly increases `totalBorrow` and `userBorrowPart[to]` for any target address, but it never transfers MIM to that borrower and it bypasses the normal borrow flow's borrow-cap checks and opening-fee accounting. The only protection is a post-write solvency check that relies on the cached `exchangeRate`.
impact: A malicious or compromised master owner can impose debt on users who never received any loan proceeds, then liquidate their collateral or strand them with synthetic liabilities. This is a direct collateral-confiscation primitive in `PrivilegedCauldronV4`, not merely a governance parameter risk.
paths: Owner calls `addBorrowPosition(victim, amount)` up to the victim's collateral limit -> victim now owes MIM they never received -> attacker or accomplice liquidates the victim's collateral
Owner uses a stale favorable cached `exchangeRate` when calling `addBorrowPosition()` -> victim is assigned even more debt than a fresh oracle would allow
